channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Crew Rescued After U.S. Helicopter Goes Down Near Strait of Hormuz
item.description: It was not immediately clear what led to the incident, which came at a time when the cease-fire in the war has grown tenuous.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: A U.S. Army Apache helicopter went down near the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: San Francisco Voters Appear to Reject Tax Hike on Highly Paid C.E.O.s
item.description: The proposal was seen as a possible measure of how residents felt about the recent flood of money into the city as a result of the A.I. boom.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: Updated voting results on Monday showed that about 46 percent of voters had approved the measure.

item.title: Nithya Raman Secures Second LA Mayor Spot, Ending Spencer Pratt’s Run
item.description: Nithya Raman, a progressive Democrat who entered the mayoral race at the last minute, secured the second spot to face Mayor Karen Bass in November. Her comeback sets up an intraparty battle.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Hubler
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Trump Previews Fall Strategy With Baseless Claims of California Vote Fraud
item.description: The president is using the slow count of mail ballots in California to renew his effort to cast doubt on election outcomes he doesn’t like, despite a lack of evidence of any widespread fraud.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Swan and Maggie Haberman
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: By baselessly framing the rise of a candidate for Los Angeles mayor as a Democratic scam, President Trump has extended his long-running project to erode public faith in elections.

item.title: Three Questions About the Maine and South Carolina Primaries
item.description: Graham Platner and Lindsey Graham are both hoping for comfortably big victories.
item.dc:creator: Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: Joe Raedle/Getty Images

item.title: Pentagon Again Revises Religious Categories for Troops
item.description: The Defense Department made the change after lawmakers objected to its original list, which did not include the Latter-day Saints among traditions labeled Christian.
item.dc:creator: John Ismay
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
Mormons (Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-Day Saints)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Mike Lee of Utah objected to a Pentagon policy memo excluding Mormons from being categorized as Christian.

item.title: Trump Wants to Call the Shots. But in Iran, He Keeps Hitting His Limits.
item.description: President Trump is grappling with his own version of the sort of Middle East crisis that beset his predecessors, and that he promised to avoid.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump insists that he has not violated his campaign pledge to avoid “endless wars,” even though a conflict he called “a little excursion” in March has entered its fourth month.

item.title: A Timeline of Donald Trump and Lindsey Graham’s Relationship Before the South Carolina Primary
item.description: Once one of President Trump’s fiercest critics, the South Carolina senator has dramatically shifted his posture over the years. Will it pay off on Tuesday in his primary?
item.dc:creator: Bayliss Wagner and Meg Felling
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times, Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Lindsey Graham of South Carolina, left, and President Trump.

item.title: Trump Nominates Blanche for Attorney General, Setting Up Confirmation Fight
item.description: As acting attorney general, Todd Blanche has shown a willingness to execute the president’s maximalist demands. Whether the Senate will confirm him remains unclear.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett and Glenn Thrush
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche’s role in the attempted creation of President Trump’s $1.8 billion payout fund gave rise to tensions with Senate Republicans, who will now have to decide whether to confirm him.

item.title: Netanyahu Called Off Iran Strikes After Trump Claimed Progress in Nuclear Talks, Officials Say
item.description: Multiple officials said that the president told Israel’s leader that the United States and Iran were within days of a breakthrough that would clear the way for talks on a long-term nuclear deal.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman, Jonathan Swan and Ronen Bergman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump has repeatedly said that the United States and Iran were coming close to a deal to end the conflict.

item.title: Hiker, 18, Dies at Grand Canyon National Park Amid High Heat
item.description: The man was with his father on a popular, strenuous trail on a day that temperatures reached above 100 degrees in parts of the canyon, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai
item.media:credit: Bridget Bennett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Hikers along Bright Angel Trail on the South Rim in Grand Canyon Village, Ariz., last year.

item.title: Ken Paxton’s Former Defense Lawyer Endorses James Talarico
item.description: The lawyer, Dan Cogdell, helped save Mr. Paxton from criminal charges and an impeachment, but now he says the Texas attorney general has “lost sight of his mission.”
item.dc:creator: J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dan Cogdell, a defense attorney for Ken Paxton, at the State Capitol in Austin during Mr. Paxton’s impeachment trial in 2023.

item.title: What’s Taking So Long to Count California Ballots?
item.description: Other states with vote-by-mail manage to count much more quickly.
item.dc:creator: Maia Spoto
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Election workers processing ballots for the California state primary at the Los Angeles County Ballot Processing Center on Friday in City of Industry, Calif.

item.title: Prosecutors Won’t Seek Death Penalty in Killing of Melissa Hortman and Husband
item.description: The Justice Department said it likely did not have legal grounds for capital punishment in the case of a man accused of killing Melissa Hortman, a Minnesota legislator, and her husband.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Melissa and Mark Hortman were killed in their home by Vance Boelter, federal prosecutors say.

item.title: Judge Throws Out Trump Administration’s Policy Imposing $100,000 Fees for H-1B Visas
item.description: The ruling voided “in its entirety” a policy from September requiring companies to pay $100,000 fees for H-1B visa petitions.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Brian Snyder/Reuters
item.media:description: Judge Leo T. Sorokin wrote that the Trump administration’s policy appeared to step on Congress’s “exclusive power” to levy taxes.

item.title: Meta Says Israeli Spyware Firm NSO Group Targeted WhatsApp Users Again
item.description: The uncovered hacking attempts on the messaging service were linked to NSO Group and may violate a federal court prohibition against the firm.
item.dc:creator: Dustin Volz
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Amit Elkayam for The New York Times
item.media:description: Offices belonging to NSO Group in Sapir, Israel. It has long been seen as a poster child for a largely unregulated spyware industry that caters to the needs of authoritarian regimes.

item.title: Trump Administration Seeks to Revoke U.S. Citizenship of 17 Immigrants
item.description: The push to denaturalize more immigrants is the latest sign that the administration is setting its sights on the legal immigration system.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: “We will continue to use every lawful avenue to denaturalize and remove aliens,” Markwayne Mullin, the homeland security secretary, said in a statement.

item.title: In Maine, Supporters of Graham Platner Continue to Back His Senate Campaign, With ‘Trepidation’
item.description: Many Democrats are sticking by their presumptive Senate nominee Graham Platner. But some have soured, and others are anxious about how recent revelations could affect a close race.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk, Alicia Anstead and Kathleen Tomaselli
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Graham Platner’s campaign has drawn large crowds of enthusiastic supporters in Maine, but many Democrats are anxious about his candidacy.

item.title: How the Drive to Find a Conspiracy Against Trump Rocked the Justice Dept.
item.description: The push to investigate what the president’s allies saw as a “deep state” cabal intent on taking him down set off cascading crises, ended careers and undercut the department’s credibility with judges.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The F.B.I. director Kash Patel’s “grand conspiracy case” sought to tie together actions by a group of people that President Trump blamed for various investigations into him.

item.title: Two New Studies Ask: Did the iPhone Cause Birthrates to Decline?
item.description: Modern smartphones rolled out in 2007, the year that fertility rates began falling. Two studies say that is not a coincidence.
item.dc:creator: Sabrina Tavernise
item.media:credit: Colin Clark for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fertility is not just dropping in the United States. The mystery is now a near-global phenomenon.

item.title: They Got Platner’s Endorsement for Maine Governor. What Did They Do With It?
item.description: The crowded race for governor has barely qualified as background noise in recent months, drowned out by the high-stakes, turbulent campaign for U.S. Senate.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell
item.media:credit: Amanda Sabga/Reuters; Cliff Owen & Joel Page, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Democratic candidates Troy Jackson, a former president of the State Senate; Shenna Bellows, the secretary of state, and Hannah Pingree, a former speaker of the State House.

item.title: Want an A.I. Degree? Here’s What You Should Think About.
item.description: The programs are popping up on campuses across America. What they teach varies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: More universities are offering A.I. degrees, hoping they’ll attract students and make their graduates more marketable.

item.title: Shootout in Olde Town Square: A Cop, a Killer and a Good Guy With a Gun
item.description: No community was better prepared for active shooters than Arvada, Colo. Then men with guns converged in the city center, each on different missions.
item.dc:creator: Mike Baker
item.media:credit: Helen H. Richardson/The Denver Post, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Police at the scene of a shooting in Arvada, Colo., in June 2021.

item.title: A.I. Degree Programs Surge as Colleges Seek Students and Relevance
item.description: Colleges from North Dakota to New Jersey are trying to get students to sign up for A.I. degrees. What they teach varies widely.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Tim Gruber for The New York Times
item.media:description: University of North Dakota is the flagship school in a state that will soon have two universities offering undergraduate or graduate degrees in A.I.
